perturbation.original_text: As used herein, "Confidential Information" shall be in written, graphic, machine recognizable or other tangible or electronic form and marked "Confidential" or "Proprietary" or shown by implication that it is imparted or disclosed in confidence, or if disclosed orally or visually, shall be reduced to writing in summary form, identified as "Confidential Information" and sent to the Receiving Party within 15 days following such oral or visual disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, "Confidential Information" shall be in written, graphic, machine recognizable or other tangible or electronic form. Information disclosed orally or visually need not be reduced to writing to be considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by removing the requirement for oral or visual disclosures to be reduced to writing to be considered Confidential Information. Initially, all oral/visual disclosures needed to be written and sent within 15 days, now oral/visual disclosures are immediately confidential without needing documentation. This contradicts section 6, which requires the return or destruction of 'all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof', creating uncertainty about the handling and verification of non-written, oral/visual data. It is now unclear on how one can return or destroy information that was not put in written form.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon the written request of either party, the other party shall promptly return to the disclosing party all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof or at the disclosing party's option shall destroy all such Confidential Information and shall provide the receiving party with a certificate that all Confidential Information has been destroyed.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for oral/visual disclosures of confidential information to be reduced to writing. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text', which mandates the return or destruction of 'all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof'. Since oral/visual disclosures are no longer required to be written down, it creates a contradiction as to how the receiving party can return or destroy information that has not been documented. This is an in-text contradiction according to the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties except after receiving prior consent by the disclosing party in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Company and BUYER agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in reasonable confidence and may disclose such Confidential Information to consultants and advisors without prior consent.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to keep information confidential. Initially, disclosure to third parties required prior written consent. Now, disclosure to consultants and advisors is permitted without consent, undermining the 'strict confidence' requirement and potentially conflicting with the purpose outlined in section 3 regarding use of confidential information for intended purposes only.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Company and BUYER agree that they will not use the other party's Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the intended purposes, without the prior written permission of the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original requirement of 'strict confidence' and prior written consent for disclosure to third parties. It now allows disclosure to consultants and advisors without prior consent. This directly contradicts the section stating that confidential information cannot be used for any purpose other than intended purposes 'without the prior written permission of the other party.' The changed text creates a direct conflict by permitting disclosure without consent, while the contradicted text requires consent for any use beyond the intended purpose, thus there is an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company and BUYER agree that they will not use the other party's Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the intended purposes, without the prior written permission of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Company and BUYER agree that they may use the other party's Confidential Information for related purposes, even if not explicitly intended, without the need for prior written permission.
perturbation.explanation: This modifies the restriction on using Confidential Information. Previously, use was strictly limited to 'intended purposes' with prior written permission needed for other uses. The revised text permits use for 'related purposes' without permission, creating ambiguity about what constitutes a 'related' purpose and undermining the original intent of restricting information usage. This contradicts section 2 where it establishes that Company and BUYER agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties except after receiving prior consent by the disclosing party in writing.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties except after receiving prior consent by the disclosing party in writing. Company and BUYER shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information as each employs with respect to its own proprietary information of like importance or a greater degree if reasonable.
perturbation.justification: The original text strictly limited the use of Confidential Information to 'intended purposes' with prior written permission for other uses. The changed text permits use for 'related purposes' without permission, which directly contradicts the contradicted text stating that Company and BUYER mutually agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties except after receiving prior consent by the disclosing party in writing. The use of 'related purposes' introduces ambiguity and undermines the confidentiality agreement, creating a direct conflict and ambiguity as to what constitutes a 'related' purpose.

perturbation.original_text: Information shall not be deemed Confidential Information and the receiving party shall have no obligation regarding any information for which it can be proven in written documentation (a) is already known to the receiving party at the time that it is disclosed without use of the Confidential Information; (b) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act contrary to this Agreement of the receiving party; (c) is rightfully received from a third party without obligation of confidence or restriction on disclosure from receiving party and without breach of this Agreement; (d) is independently developed by the receiving party without use of Confidential Information; (f) is disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a valid court order provided that the Receiving Party provides (i) prior written notice for the disclosing party of such obligation and (ii) the opportunity to oppose such disclosure and (iii) it is disclosed for the extent and purposes or the order only.
perturbation.changed_text: Information shall not be deemed Confidential Information and the receiving party shall have no obligation regarding any information for which (a) is already known to the receiving party at the time that it is disclosed without use of the Confidential Information; (b) is or becomes publicly known; (c) is rightfully received from a third party; (d) is independently developed by the receiving party; (f) is disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a valid court order, even without providing prior notice or opportunity to oppose. No written documentation is required to prove these exceptions.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the 'written documentation' requirement for proving exceptions to Confidential Information, and also removes the prior notice requirements related to valid court orders. The lack of documentation requirement creates ambiguity, as it becomes difficult to verify if the exception is valid, directly contradicting the original requirements of the agreement for maintaining confidentiality, particularly conflicting with section 6 that needs proof of destruction of Confidential Information. Also removing the prior notice requirements about valid court orders, affect the opposing party in section 2, which requires prior consent for disclosing to a third party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon the written request of either party, the other party shall promptly return to the disclosing party all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof or at the disclosing party's option shall destroy all such Confidential Information and shall provide the receiving party with a certificate that all Confidential Information has been destroyed.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for written documentation to prove exceptions to Confidential Information. The contradicted text states that upon written request, the receiving party shall return or destroy all Confidential Information and provide a certificate that all Confidential Information has been destroyed. By removing the 'written documentation' requirement to prove exceptions, it becomes unclear which information the receiving party is obligated to return or destroy, thus creating a contradiction and hindering the enforcement of the original clause in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations undertaken by each party pursuant to this Agreement shall remain in effect for three years from the last date of disclosure of Confidential Information, and shall survive any termination or expiration hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations undertaken by each party pursuant to this Agreement shall remain in effect indefinitely, unless either party provides 30 days written notice of termination. This clause supersedes any other clause regarding duration.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement set a fixed duration of three years. The modification introduces an indefinite duration with a termination clause, creating a direct contradiction. This also contradicts section 6 related to the required return or destruction of confidential information, as indefinite confidentially will prevent the request of destruction in the first place.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon the written request of either party, the other party shall promptly return to the disclosing party all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof or at the disclosing party's option shall destroy all such Confidential Information and shall provide the receiving party with a certificate that all Confidential Information has been destroyed.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the duration of the confidentiality obligations from a fixed three years to an indefinite period with a 30-day termination notice. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which requires the return or destruction of confidential information upon written request. The indefinite duration, introduced by the changed text, prevents the request of destruction because the confidentiality would still apply, thus creating a strong and clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California without regard to the conflict of laws provisions and shall be subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, and any disputes shall be resolved through binding arbitration in New York City.
perturbation.explanation: This change completely alters the governing law and dispute resolution mechanism. The original agreement specified California law and courts; the modified version specifies Delaware law and binding arbitration in New York, thus establishing a conflict of jurisdiction and legal framework. This undermines the enforceability of the contract, especially if prior clauses refer to specific California laws. It is now unclear which jurisdiction can oversee possible violations and litigations, creating an internal contradiction with the sections about confidentially and legal responsabilities.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties except after receiving prior consent by the disclosing party in writing. Company and BUYER shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information as each employs with respect to its own proprietary information of like importance or a greater degree if reasonable.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies California law and courts for governing the agreement. The changed text replaces this with Delaware law and binding arbitration in New York City. The contradicted text refers to the handling of confidential information, which would typically be interpreted under the original jurisdiction (California). The change creates a contradiction because it's now unclear under which jurisdiction the confidentiality clause and related legal responsibilities should be enforced. The change in jurisdiction and dispute resolution directly conflicts with the implicit understanding within the confidentiality clause, creating uncertainty and a strong contradiction as defined by the 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' perturbation type.
